text:
Quarto makes it easy to write once and publish everywhere.

No R required for a text-based website
Simple Markdown + YAML configuration
Build modern websites with navigation, themes, and interactive sections

Start the tutorial

section: What you can do with this site
text:
What you can do with this site

Learn Quarto website basics
Explore page structure and navigation
Use built-in themes and styles
Create content with interactive tabs, callouts, and code snippets

rendering instructions
tips for website projects

title: Welcome to Your Quarto Website
text:
Quick start
Create a new .qmd file, add YAML at the top, write Markdown, and run:
quarto render index.qmd
Then open the generated file in your browser, or render the whole site with:
quarto render

title: Welcome to Your Quarto Website
text:
Ready to explore?
Click the tutorial page to learn Quarto website building in a friendly, interactive format.

title: Full Quarto Tutorial
text:
Welcome! This tutorial is written for people who are completely new to Quarto and may also have no background in R.
Quarto is a tool for creating: - reports - blogs - websites - books - presentations - PDFs and Word documents
You write content in simple plain text using Markdown, and Quarto turns it into polished output.



Quarto is an open-source publishing system.
In simple words: - You write text in a file - You can add headings, lists, images, tables, and code - Quarto converts that file into HTML, PDF, Word, slides, and more
You do not need to know R to start using Quarto.
Quarto can work with: - plain Markdown only - Python - R - Julia - Observable JavaScript
If you are a beginner, you can first learn Quarto as a document writing tool without writing any code.




Quarto is useful because it helps you: - write once and create multiple output formats - keep text, code, and results in one place - create professional-looking documents - make reproducible reports - build personal websites, course notes, and books


You can use Quarto to create: - class notes - project reports - resumes - research papers - tutorials - data analysis reports - technical blogs - slide presentations





No.
This is very important.
You can use Quarto without knowing R.
There are two common ways to use Quarto:


You write Markdown and create a document. No programming needed.



Later, if you want, you can add Python or R code blocks.
So if you are a complete beginner, start with text-only documents.





To use Quarto comfortably, install the following:



Quarto
A text editor or IDE





VS Code (great for beginners)
RStudio / Positron (optional, even if you do not use R)





Python if you want to run Python code later
R if you want to run R code later
TinyTeX or TeX Live if you want to create PDFs




Go to the official site: - https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/
Download and install Quarto for your operating system.



Open your terminal and run:
quarto --version
If Quarto is installed correctly, you will see a version number.





Markdown is a very simple way to format text.
Instead of clicking buttons for bold headings or lists, you type small symbols.




# Main Title
## Section Title
### Smaller Section



**bold text**
*italic text*



- item one
- item two
- item three



1. First step
2. Second step
3. Third step



[Quarto Website](https://quarto.org)
This is one reason Quarto is beginner-friendly.






Quarto documents usually use the .qmd extension.
Create a file called:
hello.qmd
Add this content:
---
title: "My First Quarto Document"
format: html
---

# Hello

This is my first Quarto document.

## About Me

I am learning Quarto.

- It is simple
- It is beginner-friendly
- I do not need to know R to start


That section is called YAML.
It stores document settings like: - title - author - date - output format
In this example: - title sets the title of the document - format: html tells Quarto to create an HTML page





To convert a .qmd file into output, Quarto uses the word render.


Run:
quarto render hello.qmd
Quarto will create an HTML file, usually:
hello.html
Open that file in your browser.



If you use VS Code with Quarto installed, you can often preview or render directly from the editor.





A simple Quarto document has two main parts:


This controls document settings.
Example:
---
title: "Beginner Tutorial"
author: "Your Name"
date: "2026-06-12"
format: html
---



This is where you write: - headings - paragraphs - lists - images - tables - code blocks







# Heading 1
## Heading 2
### Heading 3



Just write normal text.



Leave a blank line between paragraphs.



**bold**
*italic*
`code`



> This is an important note.



---



- apple
- banana
- orange



1. Install Quarto
2. Create a file
3. Render the file





You can add an image like this:
![A sample image](images/example.png)
Explanation: - text inside [] is alternate text - text inside () is the image path
If your image is in a folder called images, the file path might be: - images/photo.jpg - images/chart.png




[Visit Quarto](https://quarto.org)
This creates a clickable link.




Simple Markdown table:
| Tool   | Purpose            |
|--------|--------------------|
| Quarto | Create documents   |
| VS Code| Edit files         |
| Python | Run code optional  |
This becomes a formatted table in the output.




Even if you do not know programming yet, it is helpful to understand the idea.
A code block is a section where you write code.


```bash
quarto render hello.qmd

This only displays code.

### Executable code block
If you have Python or R installed, Quarto can run code blocks and show results.

Example with Python:

````markdown
```python
print("Hello from Python")

If Python is available, Quarto can run it and include the output.

Again: **this is optional** for beginners.

---

## 14. A text-only beginner example

Create a file named `intro.qmd`:

```markdown
---
title: "Introduction to Quarto"
format: html
---

# Welcome

Quarto helps you create beautiful documents.

## What I learned

- Quarto uses `.qmd` files
- Markdown is easy to write
- I can create HTML pages
- I do not need R to begin

## My Next Step

I will learn how to add images and tables.
```

Render it:

```bash
quarto render intro.qmd
```

---

## 15. Creating different output formats

One powerful feature of Quarto is that you can generate different types of output.

### HTML
```yaml
format: html
```

### PDF
```yaml
format: pdf
```

### Word
```yaml
format: docx
```

### Multiple formats
```yaml
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
  docx: default
```

Then run:

```bash
quarto render yourfile.qmd
```

### Important note about PDF
Creating PDFs usually requires a LaTeX installation such as TinyTeX.

If PDF rendering fails, start with HTML first.

---

## 16. Working with sections

A bigger document can be organized into sections.

Example:

```markdown
# Chapter 1
## Topic 1.1
## Topic 1.2

# Chapter 2
## Topic 2.1
```

This structure helps readers navigate your content.

In HTML output, Quarto may also generate a table of contents.

---

## 17. Adding a table of contents

You can enable it in YAML:

```yaml
---
title: "My Tutorial"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---
```

This adds a table of contents automatically.

---

## 18. Changing theme and appearance

For HTML documents, you can choose themes.

Example:

```yaml
---
title: "Styled Document"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
---
```

Some common themes:
- cosmo
- litera
- flatly
- darkly
- minty

You can experiment and see which one you like.

---

## 19. Creating a presentation

Quarto can create slides too.

Example:

```markdown
---
title: "My First Presentation"
format: revealjs
---

# Slide 1
Welcome to my presentation.

# Slide 2
- Point one
- Point two
```

Render it and Quarto will create a slide deck.

---

## 20. Creating a website

Quarto can also build websites.

A basic website project often includes:
- `_quarto.yml`
- `index.qmd`
- other `.qmd` pages

Example `_quarto.yml`:

```yaml
project:
  type: website

website:
  title: "My Website"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: about.qmd
        text: About

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
```

Example `index.qmd`:

```markdown
---
title: "Home"
---

# Welcome

This is my Quarto website.
```

This is more advanced, but it shows how powerful Quarto is.

---

## 21. What is `_quarto.yml`?

When you work on a Quarto project with multiple files, `_quarto.yml` is the main configuration file.

It can define:
- project type
- website settings
- book settings
- output format defaults
- themes
- navigation

Think of it as the project control file.

---

## 22. How Quarto is different from Word

In Word, you usually format things visually.

In Quarto, you write simple plain text with lightweight formatting.

### Benefits of Quarto
- easier version control with Git and GitHub
- reusable documents
- cleaner structure
- can combine writing and code
- easier to publish online

At first it may feel unfamiliar, but many people find it easier over time.

---

## 23. Beginner workflow

Here is a simple beginner workflow:

1. Install Quarto
2. Create a `.qmd` file
3. Write Markdown content
4. Add YAML at the top
5. Render the file to HTML
6. Open the output in a browser
7. Edit and render again

This is enough to get started.

---

## 24. Common beginner mistakes

### Mistake 1: Forgetting the YAML lines
YAML must start and end with `---`.

### Mistake 2: Wrong file extension
Use `.qmd`, not `.txt`.

### Mistake 3: PDF not rendering
This often happens because LaTeX is not installed.

### Mistake 4: Broken image path
Make sure the image file path is correct.

### Mistake 5: Expecting code to run without installing Python or R
If you want executable code blocks, you need the related language installed.

---

## 25. Example: A small personal profile page

Create `profile.qmd`:

```markdown
---
title: "My Profile"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---

# About Me

My name is Praveen.

I am learning Quarto to create documents and websites.

## Skills I Want to Learn

- Quarto
- Markdown
- GitHub
- Python

## Favorite Resources

- [Quarto](https://quarto.org)
- [Markdown Guide](https://www.markdownguide.org)
```

Render it:

```bash
quarto render profile.qmd
```

---

## 26. Example: A study notes document

```markdown
---
title: "Study Notes"
format: html
---

# Chapter 1

This chapter explains the basics.

## Key Points

- Point A
- Point B
- Point C

## Summary

This chapter introduced the main ideas.
```

This is useful for students and self-learners.

---

## 27. If you later want to learn code in Quarto

Once you are comfortable with text and Markdown, you can gradually add code.

### Python example

````markdown
```python
name = "Praveen"
print("Hello", name)
```



```r
x <- 10
x + 5
```
But remember: learning Quarto does not require learning R first.






Start with HTML output
Write short documents first
Focus on Markdown before advanced features
Keep files organized in folders
Use meaningful file names
Render often to check your output
Learn one feature at a time





Here is a beginner-friendly order:


Learn: - headings - paragraphs - bold and italic - lists - links



Learn: - YAML title and format - rendering to HTML - adding images - adding tables



Learn: - themes - table of contents - multiple pages - websites



Optional: - Python code blocks - R code blocks - PDFs - presentations - books







No. Anyone can use it for writing and publishing.



No. You can start with plain text and Markdown.



Yes, Quarto is open source and free to use.



Yes.



Yes.



No. VS Code works very well too.





Before starting, make sure you can do these:

install Quarto
create a .qmd file
write a title in YAML
add headings and lists
render to HTML
open the output file

If you can do these, you are already using Quarto successfully.




Quarto is a beginner-friendly publishing tool.
Even if you: - do not know R - do not know programming - have never written Markdown before
You can still start learning Quarto today.
The best way to learn is: - create a small .qmd file - write simple content - render it - improve it step by step
Start simple, and build confidence gradually.




Try this today:

Install Quarto
Create hello.qmd
Paste this content:

---
title: "Hello Quarto"
format: html
---

# My First Page

I am learning Quarto.

- I can write text
- I can create headings
- I can render HTML

Run:

quarto render hello.qmd
That is your first Quarto success.

If you want, the next tutorial can be about: - Quarto for complete beginners using VS Code - How to create a Quarto website step by step - Markdown for absolute beginners - Quarto with Python for beginners

title: Quarto Tutorial for Beginners
text:
This page teaches you Quarto step-by-step. It is built for people who are new to Quarto and may not have any background in R.






Important



You do not need R to use Quarto. Start with plain Markdown and HTML output, then add code later if you want.




Quarto is an open-source publishing system that turns plain text into polished documents and websites.

Write content in Markdown
Add headings, lists, links, images, and tables
Render to HTML, PDF, Word, slides, and more




Quarto helps you:

create one document and output it in multiple formats
keep text and code together in one file
build websites, tutorials, and reports
publish clean content with version control




No.
Quarto works with:

plain Markdown only
Python
R
Julia
Observable JavaScript

For beginners, start with text-only documents.






Quarto
A text editor such as VS Code





Python or R if you want executable code blocks
A TeX engine like TinyTeX or TeX Live for PDF output




quarto --version
If Quarto is installed, this prints a version number.




Markdown is a simple text format that makes writing easy.


# Main title
## Section title
### Smaller section



**bold**
*italic*



- item one
- item two



[Quarto website](https://quarto.org)




Create a file named hello.qmd and add:
---
title: "My First Quarto Document"
format: html
---

# Hello

This is my first Quarto document.

- It is simple
- It is beginner-friendly
- I do not need to know R to start



The section between --- lines is YAML. It sets the document title, format, and other options.
Example:
---
title: "Beginner Tutorial"
format: html
---



Run this command:
quarto render hello.qmd
Quarto generates hello.html.



Quarto websites use multiple .qmd files and a _quarto.yml project file.
Example _quarto.yml:
project:
  type: website

website:
  title: "My Quarto Website"
  navbar:
    right:
      - href: index.qmd
        text: Home
      - href: tutorial.qmd
        text: Tutorial

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
Example index.qmd:
---
title: "Home"
---

# Welcome

This is my Quarto website.





![A sample image](images/example.png)



| Tool   | Purpose          |
|--------|------------------|
| Quarto | Create documents |
| VS Code| Edit files       |




You can add code blocks that only show code:
quarto render hello.qmd
If you install Python or R, Quarto can also run code and show results.




Code
print("Hello from Python")


Hello from Python






You can render HTML, PDF, and Word from one file.
format:
  html: default
  pdf: default
  docx: default




Forgetting the --- lines in YAML
Using the wrong file extension (.qmd instead of .txt)
Broken image paths
Trying to run code without installing Python or R





Install Quarto
Create a .qmd file
Add YAML and Markdown
Render to HTML
View the output
Edit and render again




Try these next steps:

Add another page like about.qmd
Use a custom theme or CSS
Add images, tables, and links
Experiment with format: docx or format: pdf


Great! Your Quarto website now contains a full beginner tutorial with examples and step-by-step guidance.

title: Quarto Tutorial for Beginners
text:
Quarto is an open-source publishing system that turns plain text into polished documents and websites.

Write content in Markdown
Add headings, lists, links, images, and tables
Render to HTML, PDF, Word, slides, and more

title: Quarto Tutorial for Beginners
text:
Quarto helps you:

create one document and output it in multiple formats
keep text and code together in one file
build websites, tutorials, and reports
publish clean content with version control

title: Quarto Tutorial for Beginners
text:
No.
Quarto works with:

plain Markdown only
Python
R
Julia
Observable JavaScript

For beginners, start with text-only documents.

title: Quarto Tutorial for Beginners
text:
Quarto
A text editor such as VS Code





Python or R if you want executable code blocks
A TeX engine like TinyTeX or TeX Live for PDF output




quarto --version
If Quarto is installed, this prints a version number.

title: Quarto Tutorial for Beginners
text:

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
Nextflow is a workflow DSL that makes it easy to write scalable and reproducible pipelines that run locally or on cloud providers (GCP, AWS). It integrates well with containers, Conda, R and Python tools, and supports parallel execution out of the box.






Important



This guide focuses on practical, runnable examples: local testing, GCP and AWS execution, and integrating R/Python code. Replace example placeholders (project IDs, bucket names, IAM ARNs) with your values.

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
Nextflow is a workflow DSL that makes it easy to write scalable and reproducible pipelines that run locally or on cloud providers (GCP, AWS). It integrates well with containers, Conda, R and Python tools, and supports parallel execution out of the box.






Important



This guide focuses on practical, runnable examples: local testing, GCP and AWS execution, and integrating R/Python code. Replace example placeholders (project IDs, bucket names, IAM ARNs) with your values.

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
section: 2. Quick Start — install and run locally
text:
2. Quick Start — install and run locally

Requirements

Java 11+ (Nextflow runs on the JVM)
nextflow CLI (install via curl)
Optional: Docker or Conda for reproducible software environments

# Install Nextflow (Linux/macOS)
curl -s https://get.nextflow.io | bash
# Move to PATH (example)
sudo mv nextflow /usr/local/bin/

# Verify
nextflow -v


Hello-world workflow (main.nf)
#!/usr/bin/env nextflow

process sayHello {
  output:
    file 'hello.txt'

  script:
  """
  echo "Hello from Nextflow" > hello.txt
  """
}

workflow {
  sayHello()
}
Run locally:
nextflow run main.nf -profile standard
ls -l work/
cat work/*/hello.txt

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
3. Core Concepts (brief)

process: computational step (command/script) with input/output.
channel: stream of data connecting processes.
workflow: organizes processes and channels.

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
section: 4. Running Nextflow on Google Cloud Platform (GCP)
text:
4. Running Nextflow on Google Cloud Platform (GCP)

Overview
We demonstrate using GCP Compute + Cloud Storage. For larger work, you can use Nextflow Tower, Google Genomics Pipelines, or compute clusters (GKE).


Prerequisites

gcloud installed and authenticated
A GCP project and billing enabled
A GCS bucket for workspace/storage

# Set project and region
gcloud config set project YOUR_PROJECT_ID
gcloud config set compute/region us-central1
# Create bucket (example)
gsutil mb -l us-central1 gs://your-nextflow-bucket


Nextflow config for GCP (example nextflow.config)
profiles {
  gcp {
    process.executor = 'google-lifesciences'  // uses Google Life Sciences API
    google {
      project = 'YOUR_PROJECT_ID'
      region  = 'us-central1'
      auth = '/path/to/google-credentials.json'   // or use ADC
      bucket = 'gs://your-nextflow-bucket'
    }
    workDir = 'gs://your-nextflow-bucket/work'
  }
}

Authentication: prefer Application Default Credentials (ADC). To create a service account and key:

gcloud iam service-accounts create nf-runner --display-name "Nextflow runner"
gcloud projects add-iam-policy-binding YOUR_PROJECT_ID \
  --member="serviceAccount:nf-runner@YOUR_PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com" \
  --role="roles/storage.admin"
gcloud iam service-accounts keys create key.json \
  --iam-account nf-runner@YOUR_PROJECT_ID.iam.gserviceaccount.com
export GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS="$(pwd)/key.json"


Run on GCP
nextflow run main.nf -profile gcp -with-report report.html -with-trace trace.txt


Cost & monitoring

Store intermediate files in GCS to avoid expensive disk.
Use small VM images for lightweight tasks.
Monitor via Cloud Console and check gcloud billing reports.

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
6. Integrating R and Python

Containerization (recommended)

Use Docker images with R and Python environments, or use Conda with Nextflow’s conda support.

Example process using Python:
process runPython {
  container 'python:3.11-slim'
  input:
    path x
  output:
    path 'out.txt'

  script:
  """
  python -c 'print("Processing", "$x")' > out.txt
  """
}
Example conda usage:
process analysis {
  conda 'envs/env.yml'
  script:
  """
  Rscript analysis.R input.csv output.csv
  """
}
envs/env.yml (example)
name: nf-env
channels:
  - conda-forge
dependencies:
  - r-base=4.2
  - r-essentials
  - python=3.11
  - pandas


R script example (analysis.R)
args <- commandArgs(trailingOnly = TRUE)
dat <- read.csv(args[1])
summary <- summary(dat)
write.csv(as.data.frame(summary), args[2], row.names=FALSE)


Passing data between R and Python

Use files in workDir and channels to pass file paths.
Keep intermediate files in object storage (S3/GCS) when running in cloud.

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
7. Practical Bioinformatics Example (FASTQ QC & alignment)

Process 1: split/validate FASTQ
Process 2: run fastp or fastqc
Process 3: align with bwa or minimap2
Use containers for exact tool versions and pin references.

Small example process:
process fastqc {
  container 'biocontainers/fastqc:v0.11.9_cv8'
  input:
    path fastq
  output:
    path "*.html"
  """
  fastqc $fastq -o .
  """
}

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
8. Clinical Trials / Regulatory Considerations

Enforce data access controls: use private buckets and VPCs.
Log and version workflows: enable reports and execution metadata.
Separate PHI/PII and use encryption at rest/in transit.

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
9. Best Practices

Containerize reproducible environments (Docker/Conda).
Test workflows locally before the cloud.
Use small sample datasets for CI tests.
Keep credentials out of repo; use secret managers or ADC.

title: Nextflow: Quick Guide for Bioinformatics & Cloud
text:
10. References & Next Steps

Nextflow docs: https://www.nextflow.io/docs/
Nextflow + Google: https://www.nextflow.io/docs/latest/GoogleCloud.html
Nextflow + AWS: https://www.nextflow.io/docs/latest/aws/index.html
